title: S-arithmetic Diophantine approximation
speaker: Shreyasi Datta
abstract: Diophantine approximation deals with quantitative and qualitative aspects of approximating numbers by rationals. A major breakthrough by Kleinbock and Margulis in 1998 was to study Diophantine approximations for manifolds using homogeneous dynamics. After giving an overview of recent developments in this subject, I will talk about Diophantine approximation in the S-arithmetic set-up, where S is a finite set of valuations of rationals.

proof_stages.label: Dirichlet approximation and modes of improvement
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up real and p-adic Dirichlet-type inequalities, explains why numerator bounds differ in the p-adic setting, and distinguishes asymptotic approximation by a function from uniform improvement.

proof_stages.label: Dani correspondence in real and p-adic settings
proof_stages.summary: Real approximation is translated into cusp excursions of diagonal orbits in SL(2,R)/SL(2,Z), while the p-adic analogue requires an S-arithmetic space such as SL(2,Q_p)×SL(2,R)/SL(2,Z[1/p]) and a dynamically adapted approximation notion.

proof_stages.label: Nullity problem for very well approximable points on manifolds
proof_stages.summary: After proving by Borel-Cantelli that the real very well approximable set has Lebesgue measure zero, the speaker explains why manifolds need a nonplanarity condition and recalls the Kleinbock-Margulis theorem proving Sprindžuk's conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Cusp-measure estimates behind the Kleinbock-Margulis proof
proof_stages.summary: The manifold VWA problem is reduced via dynamics to estimating the measure of parameters for which δ(g_tu_{f(x)}Λ) is small, with a quantitative bound Cε^α|B| enabling Borel-Cantelli.

proof_stages.label: Khintchine, fractal, and S-adic extensions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys replacement of power-law improvement by general approximating functions, fractal-measure analogues, and existing p-adic/S-adic convergence and divergence results of Kleinbock-Margulis type.

proof_stages.label: New inhomogeneous S-arithmetic and p-adic fractal theorems
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated: an inhomogeneous S-arithmetic Khintchine-type theorem with convergence and partial divergence statements, and a p-adic analogue of the Kleinbock-Lindenstrauss-Weiss fractal-measure theorem.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative nondivergence mechanism
proof_stages.summary: To indicate the proof, the speaker reviews Margulis nondivergence, explains the SL(2) small-vector picture, generalizes to primitive subgroups in higher rank, and states the quantitative nondivergence criterion and its fractal-measure version.

proof_stages.label: p-adic goodness without a mean-value theorem
proof_stages.summary: The final proof difficulty is to verify the required goodness conditions over Q_p, where the real mean-value theorem used in the fractal nondivergence argument is unavailable and must be replaced by divided-difference conditions.

examples.name: Rational diagonal line y = x in R^2
examples.note: The rational line makes an integer linear form vanish identically on the manifold, showing why a flat/rationally dependent manifold cannot satisfy the ambient null VWA conclusion and motivating the nonplanarity condition.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the specific line y=x and checks that rational dependence of the coordinates makes the Diophantine expression vanish, so every point is very well approximable

passes.reason: a flat rational affine subspace with the relation x-y=0 is used to show the ambient measure-zero statement fails for arbitrary manifolds
A rational affine line has dependent coordinates and is used to show the naive measure-zero statement for arbitrary curves fails.
The rational diagonal is a flat rationally dependent submanifold, used to show the manifold measure-zero statement fails without a nonplanarity condition.
It is a rationally degenerate affine line, used to show the zero-measure VWA statement fails for arbitrary manifolds.
A rational affine line is flat/rationally dependent and is used to show the naive measure-zero statement for all manifolds fails.
The rational diagonal is a flat rationally dependent submanifold, used to show the zero-measure VWA conclusion fails without a nonplanarity condition.
A flat rational affine line with rationally dependent coordinates is used to show the naive zero-measure manifold statement fails.
used to show the zero-measure VWA conclusion fails for rationally flat submanifolds, since the diagonal has an exact rational linear relation.
A rational flat affine line has a linear dependence making all its points very well approximable, so it is used to refute the naive manifold-measure-zero claim.
used to show the naive measure-zero statement for arbitrary manifolds fails because the rational flat line has dependent coordinates
used to refute the naive manifold measure-zero claim via a flat rational affine line with dependent coordinates
Used to show the manifold statement fails without nonplanarity, and the rational affine relation makes the line Diophantinely degenerate.

examples.name: Parabola in Mahler-Sprindžuk theory
examples.note: The parabola is given as the concrete curved case behind Mahler's question and Sprindžuk's conjectural extension to nondegenerate manifolds.
examples.engagement_reason: the parabola is cited as Mahler’s proved case and as motivation for the Sprindžuk conjecture, without any calculation or verification on the parabola itself

examples.name: Natural Hausdorff measure on the Cantor set
examples.note: An audience question asks whether the uniform Hausdorff measure on the Cantor set is an allowed fractal measure, clarifying the class of measures in the Kleinbock-Lindenstrauss-Weiss type theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the Cantor-set Hausdorff measure is only accepted as a legal example of a fractal measure, with no construction or property checked on it

examples.name: SL(2,R)/SL(2,Z) toy case for nondivergence
examples.note: The two-dimensional lattice case shows how small-vector intervals are disjoint and leads to the higher-dimensional idea of replacing short vectors by short primitive subgroups.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the SL(2,R)/SL(2,Z) case by reasoning about unimodular lattices, small vectors, primitive vectors, and estimating the bad set via disjoint intervals

passes.reason: the n=2 homogeneous-space quotient is the minimal toy case for the general nondivergence argument

The SL(2) quotient is the minimal toy/base case of the lattice-space nondivergence argument, used illustratively.
